question:
8. For the family of parabolas
\[
y=\frac{a^{3} x^{2}}{3}+\frac{a^{2} x}{2}-2 a,
\]
(i) find the locus of vertices,
(ii) find the envelope,
(iii) sketch the envelope and two typical curves of the family.
solution:
Solution. (i) The given equation can be written in standard form as
\[
y+\frac{35}{16} a=\frac{a^{3}}{3}\left(x+\frac{3}{4 a}\right)^{2}
\]
whence a typical vertex is
\[
\left(-\frac{3}{4 a},-\frac{35}{16} a\right)
\]

If \( a=0 \), then the given curve is a straight line, not a parabola, and therefore it has no vertex. The vertices of the parabolas in the system all lie on the hyperbola \( x y=105 / 64 \).

Conversely every point of this hyperbola is the vertex of a unique member of this family, since if \( \left(x_{0}, y_{0}\right) \) is on the hyperbola, then \( x_{0}=-3 / 4 a \), \( y_{0}=-35 a / 16 \) can be solved uniquely for \( a \).
(ii) Let
\[
f(x, y, a)=\frac{a^{3} x^{2}}{3}+\frac{a^{2} x}{2}-2 a-y
\]

Then
\[
\frac{\partial f}{\partial a}(x, y, a)=(a x+2)(a x-1)
\]

To find the envelope of the family, we eliminate \( a \) between \( \partial f / \partial a=0 \) and \( f=0 \). This gives
\[
x y=\frac{(a x)^{3}}{3}+\frac{(a x)^{2}}{2}-2 a x=\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{2}-2=\frac{-7}{6}
\]
or
\[
=\frac{-8}{3}+\frac{4}{2}+4=\frac{10}{3}
\]
depending on which of the two factors of \( \partial f / \partial a \) we choose to equate to zero.

We can readily verify that the parabola corresponding to the parameter \( a \) is tangent to the hyperbola
\[
x y=-7 / 6 \text { at }\left(\frac{1}{a},-\frac{7 a}{6}\right)
\]
and tangent to the hyperbola
\[
x y=10 / 3 \text { at }\left(-\frac{2}{a},-\frac{5}{3} a\right) .
\]

Hence the envelope is the union of the two hyperbolas.

descriptive_long.question:
8. For the family of parabolas
\[
ordinate=\frac{paracon^{3} abscis^{2}}{3}+\frac{paracon^{2} abscis}{2}-2 paracon,
\]
(i) find the locus of vertices,
(ii) find the envelope,
(iii) sketch the envelope and two typical curves of the family.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. (i) The given equation can be written in standard form as
\[
ordinate+\frac{35}{16} paracon=\frac{paracon^{3}}{3}\left(abscis+\frac{3}{4 paracon}\right)^{2}
\]
whence a typical vertex is
\[
\left(-\frac{3}{4 paracon},-\frac{35}{16} paracon\right)
\]

If \( paracon=0 \), then the given curve is a straight line, not a parabola, and therefore it has no vertex. The vertices of the parabolas in the system all lie on the hyperbola \( abscis \, ordinate=105 / 64 \).

Conversely every point of this hyperbola is the vertex of a unique member of this family, since if \( \left(abscisnaught, ordinatenaught\right) \) is on the hyperbola, then \( abscisnaught=-3 / 4 paracon \), \( ordinatenaught=-35 paracon / 16 \) can be solved uniquely for \( paracon \).
(ii) Let
\[
f(abscis, ordinate, paracon)=\frac{paracon^{3} \, abscis^{2}}{3}+\frac{paracon^{2} \, abscis}{2}-2 paracon-ordinate
\]

Then
\[
\frac{\partial f}{\partial paracon}(abscis, ordinate, paracon)=(paracon \, abscis+2)(paracon \, abscis-1)
\]

To find the envelope of the family, we eliminate \( paracon \) between \( \partial f / \partial paracon=0 \) and \( f=0 \). This gives
\[
abscis \, ordinate=\frac{(paracon \, abscis)^{3}}{3}+\frac{(paracon \, abscis)^{2}}{2}-2 paracon \, abscis=\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{2}-2=\frac{-7}{6}
\]
or
\[
=\frac{-8}{3}+\frac{4}{2}+4=\frac{10}{3}
\]
depending on which of the two factors of \( \partial f / \partial paracon \) we choose to equate to zero.

We can readily verify that the parabola corresponding to the parameter \( paracon \) is tangent to the hyperbola
\[
abscis \, ordinate=-7 / 6 \text { at }\left(\frac{1}{paracon},-\frac{7 paracon}{6}\right)
\]
and tangent to the hyperbola
\[
abscis \, ordinate=10 / 3 \text { at }\left(-\frac{2}{paracon},-\frac{5}{3} paracon\right) .
\]

Hence the envelope is the union of the two hyperbolas.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
8. For the family of parabolas
\[
pineapple=\frac{marathon^{3} sunflower^{2}}{3}+\frac{marathon^{2} sunflower}{2}-2 marathon,
\]
(i) find the locus of vertices,
(ii) find the envelope,
(iii) sketch the envelope and two typical curves of the family.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. (i) The given equation can be written in standard form as
\[
pineapple+\frac{35}{16} marathon=\frac{marathon^{3}}{3}\left(sunflower+\frac{3}{4 marathon}\right)^{2}
\]
whence a typical vertex is
\[
\left(-\frac{3}{4 marathon},-\frac{35}{16} marathon\right)
\]

If \( marathon=0 \), then the given curve is a straight line, not a parabola, and therefore it has no vertex. The vertices of the parabolas in the system all lie on the hyperbola \( sunflower pineapple=105 / 64 \).

Conversely every point of this hyperbola is the vertex of a unique member of this family, since if \( \left(rainstorm, butterfly\right) \) is on the hyperbola, then \( rainstorm=-3 / 4 marathon \), \( butterfly=-35 marathon / 16 \) can be solved uniquely for \( marathon \).

(ii) Let
\[
f(sunflower, pineapple, marathon)=\frac{marathon^{3} sunflower^{2}}{3}+\frac{marathon^{2} sunflower}{2}-2 marathon-pineapple
\]

Then
\[
\frac{\partial f}{\partial marathon}(sunflower, pineapple, marathon)=(marathon sunflower+2)(marathon sunflower-1)
\]

To find the envelope of the family, we eliminate \( marathon \) between \( \partial f / \partial marathon=0 \) and \( f=0 \). This gives
\[
sunflower pineapple=\frac{(marathon sunflower)^{3}}{3}+\frac{(marathon sunflower)^{2}}{2}-2 marathon sunflower=\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{2}-2=\frac{-7}{6}
\]
or
\[
=\frac{-8}{3}+\frac{4}{2}+4=\frac{10}{3}
\]
depending on which of the two factors of \( \partial f / \partial marathon \) we choose to equate to zero.

We can readily verify that the parabola corresponding to the parameter \( marathon \) is tangent to the hyperbola
\[
sunflower pineapple=-7 / 6 \text { at }\left(\frac{1}{marathon},-\frac{7 marathon}{6}\right)
\]
and tangent to the hyperbola
\[
sunflower pineapple=10 / 3 \text { at }\left(-\frac{2}{marathon},-\frac{5}{3} marathon\right) .
\]

Hence the envelope is the union of the two hyperbolas.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
8. For the family of parabolas
\[
horizontalaxis=\frac{resultvalue^{3} verticalaxis^{2}}{3}+\frac{resultvalue^{2} verticalaxis}{2}-2 resultvalue,
\]
(i) find the locus of vertices,
(ii) find the envelope,
(iii) sketch the envelope and two typical curves of the family.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. (i) The given equation can be written in standard form as
\[
horizontalaxis+\frac{35}{16} resultvalue=\frac{resultvalue^{3}}{3}\left(verticalaxis+\frac{3}{4 resultvalue}\right)^{2}
\]
whence a typical vertex is
\[
\left(-\frac{3}{4 resultvalue},-\frac{35}{16} resultvalue\right)
\]

If \( resultvalue=0 \), then the given curve is a straight line, not a parabola, and therefore it has no vertex. The vertices of the parabolas in the system all lie on the hyperbola \( verticalaxis horizontalaxis=105 / 64 \).

Conversely every point of this hyperbola is the vertex of a unique member of this family, since if \( \left(slopevalue, interceptvalue\right) \) is on the hyperbola, then \( slopevalue=-3 / 4 resultvalue \), \( interceptvalue=-35 resultvalue / 16 \) can be solved uniquely for \( resultvalue \).
(ii) Let
\[
f(verticalaxis, horizontalaxis, resultvalue)=\frac{resultvalue^{3} verticalaxis^{2}}{3}+\frac{resultvalue^{2} verticalaxis}{2}-2 resultvalue-horizontalaxis
\]

Then
\[
\frac{\partial f}{\partial resultvalue}(verticalaxis, horizontalaxis, resultvalue)=(resultvalue verticalaxis+2)(resultvalue verticalaxis-1)
\]

To find the envelope of the family, we eliminate \( resultvalue \) between \( \partial f / \partial resultvalue=0 \) and \( f=0 \). This gives
\[
verticalaxis horizontalaxis=\frac{(resultvalue verticalaxis)^{3}}{3}+\frac{(resultvalue verticalaxis)^{2}}{2}-2 resultvalue verticalaxis=\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{2}-2=\frac{-7}{6}
\]
or
\[
=\frac{-8}{3}+\frac{4}{2}+4=\frac{10}{3}
\]
depending on which of the two factors of \( \partial f / \partial resultvalue \) we choose to equate to zero.

We can readily verify that the parabola corresponding to the parameter \( resultvalue \) is tangent to the hyperbola
\[
verticalaxis horizontalaxis=-7 / 6 \text { at }\left(\frac{1}{resultvalue},-\frac{7 resultvalue}{6}\right)
\]
and tangent to the hyperbola
\[
verticalaxis horizontalaxis=10 / 3 \text { at }\left(-\frac{2}{resultvalue},-\frac{5}{3} resultvalue\right) .
\]

Hence the envelope is the union of the two hyperbolas.

garbled_string.question:
8. For the family of parabolas
\[
hjgrksla=\frac{mcfpdqer^{3} qzxwvtnp^{2}}{3}+\frac{mcfpdqer^{2} qzxwvtnp}{2}-2 mcfpdqer,
\]
(i) find the locus of vertices,
(ii) find the envelope,
(iii) sketch the envelope and two typical curves of the family.
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. (i) The given equation can be written in standard form as
\[
hjgrksla+\frac{35}{16} mcfpdqer=\frac{mcfpdqer^{3}}{3}\left(qzxwvtnp+\frac{3}{4 mcfpdqer}\right)^{2}
\]
whence a typical vertex is
\[
\left(-\frac{3}{4 mcfpdqer},-\frac{35}{16} mcfpdqer\right)
\]

If \( mcfpdqer=0 \), then the given curve is a straight line, not a parabola, and therefore it has no vertex. The vertices of the parabolas in the system all lie on the hyperbola \( qzxwvtnp hjgrksla=105 / 64 \).

Conversely every point of this hyperbola is the vertex of a unique member of this family, since if \( \left(lkjhgfds, poiuytre\right) \) is on the hyperbola, then \( lkjhgfds=-3 / 4 mcfpdqer \), \( poiuytre=-35 mcfpdqer / 16 \) can be solved uniquely for \( mcfpdqer \).
(ii) Let
\[
f(qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla, mcfpdqer)=\frac{mcfpdqer^{3} qzxwvtnp^{2}}{3}+\frac{mcfpdqer^{2} qzxwvtnp}{2}-2 mcfpdqer-hjgrksla
\]

Then
\[
\frac{\partial f}{\partial mcfpdqer}(qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla, mcfpdqer)=(mcfpdqer qzxwvtnp+2)(mcfpdqer qzxwvtnp-1)
\]

To find the envelope of the family, we eliminate \( mcfpdqer \) between \( \partial f / \partial mcfpdqer=0 \) and \( f=0 \). This gives
\[
qzxwvtnp hjgrksla=\frac{(mcfpdqer qzxwvtnp)^{3}}{3}+\frac{(mcfpdqer qzxwvtnp)^{2}}{2}-2 mcfpdqer qzxwvtnp=\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{2}-2=\frac{-7}{6}
\]
or
\[
=\frac{-8}{3}+\frac{4}{2}+4=\frac{10}{3}
\]
depending on which of the two factors of \( \partial f / \partial mcfpdqer \) we choose to equate to zero.

We can readily verify that the parabola corresponding to the parameter \( mcfpdqer \) is tangent to the hyperbola
\[
qzxwvtnp hjgrksla=-7 / 6 \text { at }\left(\frac{1}{mcfpdqer},-\frac{7 mcfpdqer}{6}\right)
\]
and tangent to the hyperbola
\[
qzxwvtnp hjgrksla=10 / 3 \text { at }\left(-\frac{2}{mcfpdqer},-\frac{5}{3} mcfpdqer\right) .
\]

Hence the envelope is the union of the two hyperbolas.

kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
S_{a}\;:\;
z=\frac{a^{3}}{3}\bigl(x^{2}+y^{2}\bigr)
+\frac{a^{2}}{2}\,(x-2y)-6a ,
\qquad a\in\mathbb R\setminus\{0\},
\tag{$\star$}
\]
be a one-parameter family of quadratic surfaces in $\mathbb R^{3}$.  
Throughout write  
\[
r^{2}=x^{2}+y^{2},
\qquad
s=x-2y .
\]

(i)  A \emph{vertex} of $S_{a}$ is a point for which the gradient (with respect to $x,y$) of the right-hand side of $(\star)$ vanishes.  
Determine all vertices and describe their locus $\mathcal C$ both parametrically and in Cartesian form.

(ii)  Denote by $\Sigma$ the \emph{envelope} of the family
$(\star)$, i.e. the set of points that are tangent to (at least) one
member $S_{a}$.

\quad (a)  Show that $(x,y,z)\in\Sigma$ iff the system  
\[
F(x,y,z,a)=0,\qquad F_{a}(x,y,z,a)=0,\qquad
F:=z-\frac{a^{3}}{3}r^{2}-\frac{a^{2}}{2}s+6a
\tag{E}
\]
admits a real solution $a$.

\quad (b)  Solving $F_{a}=0$ for $r^{2}$ and using the polar
representation $(x,y)=(r\cos\theta,r\sin\theta)$,
obtain a smooth parametrisation
\[
X(a,\theta)=\bigl(x(a,\theta),y(a,\theta),z(a,\theta)\bigr),
\qquad a\in\mathbb R\setminus\{0\},\; \theta\in[0,2\pi),
\]
of $\Sigma$.

\quad (c)  Eliminate $a$ from the two equations in $(E)$ and show that
$\Sigma$ is the zero-locus of the irreducible sextic
\[
P(x,y,z)=
6z^{2}(x^{2}+y^{2})^{2}-z(x-2y)^{3}
-36z(x^{2}+y^{2})(x-2y)
-18(x-2y)^{2}-576(x^{2}+y^{2}).
\tag{$\dagger$}
\]

(iii)  Fix $a\neq 0$ and set
\[
\Gamma_{a}:=S_{a}\cap\Sigma .
\]

\quad (a)  Show that $\Gamma_{a}$ is a smooth closed space curve.

\quad (b)  Prove that the orthogonal projection of
$\Gamma_{a}$ onto the $(x,y)$-plane is the circle
\[
\left(x+\frac{1}{2a}\right)^{2}+
\left(y-\frac{1}{a}\right)^{2}=\frac{29}{4a^{2}},
\tag{$\diamondsuit$}
\]
and produce an explicit parametrisation of $\Gamma_{a}$ valid for all
$a\neq 0$.

\quad (c)  Combine the two defining equations of $\Gamma_{a}$ and deduce
that every point of $\Gamma_{a}$ satisfies the additional \emph{linear}
relation
\[
6z-a^{2}\bigl(x-2y\bigr)+24a=0.
\]
Hence each $\Gamma_{a}$ is contained in the plane
\[
\Pi_{a}:\quad 6z-a^{2}(x-2y)+24a=0,
\]
and therefore planar.  Show further that $\Gamma_{a}$ is not a line
(e.g.\ by using the result of (b)).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
kernel_variant.solution:
\textbf{(i)  Vertices.}  
\[
\frac{\partial z}{\partial x}
=\frac{2a^{3}}{3}x+\frac{a^{2}}{2},
\qquad
\frac{\partial z}{\partial y}
=\frac{2a^{3}}{3}y-a^{2}.
\]
Both vanish exactly when 
\[
x=-\frac{3}{4a},\qquad y=\frac{3}{2a}.
\]
Substituting in $(\star)$ gives  
\[
z=\frac{a^{3}}{3}\Bigl(\frac{9}{16a^{2}}+\frac{9}{4a^{2}}\Bigr)
    +\frac{a^{2}}{2}\Bigl(-\frac{3}{4a}-\frac{3}{a}\Bigr)-6a
  =-\frac{111}{16}\,a .
\]
Hence  
\[
\boxed{\;
\mathcal C:\;
(x,y,z)=\Bigl(-\dfrac{3}{4a},\,\dfrac{3}{2a},\,-\dfrac{111}{16}a\Bigr),
\qquad a\in\mathbb R\setminus\{0\}\;}.
\]
Eliminating $a$ yields the Cartesian description  
\[
y+2x=0,\qquad 64xz=333,
\]
a rectangular hyperbola contained in the plane $y+2x=0$.

\bigskip
\textbf{(ii)  The envelope $\Sigma$.}

\emph{(a)}  A point belongs to the envelope precisely when it lies on
some $S_{a}$ \emph{and} that surface is tangent there; the two
conditions are $F=0$ and $F_{a}=0$, i.e. system $(E)$.

\smallskip
\emph{(b)  Explicit parametrisation.}
From $F_{a}=0$ one obtains
\[
a^{2}r^{2}+as-6=0.
\tag{1}
\]
Write $(x,y)=(r\cos\theta,r\sin\theta)$ and put  
\[
k(\theta)=\cos\theta-2\sin\theta.
\]
Equation (1) is quadratic in $r$:
\[
a^{2}r^{2}+a r\,k(\theta)-6=0
\quad(r>0).
\]
With $\varepsilon=\operatorname{sgn}(a)$ its positive root is
\[
r(a,\theta)=\frac{-\varepsilon k(\theta)+\sqrt{k(\theta)^{2}+24}}{2|a|}.
\tag{2}
\]
Using $s=r k(\theta)$ and (2) we obtain the explicit map
\[
\boxed{\,%
X(a,\theta)=
\Bigl(r\cos\theta,\;
      r\sin\theta,\;
      -4a+\frac{a^{2}r\,k(\theta)}{6}\Bigr)\!,
\qquad
a\neq 0,\;0\le\theta<2\pi\,}.
\]
All three components depend smoothly on $(a,\theta)$ and
$\partial_{\theta}X,\partial_{a}X$ are linearly independent, hence $X$
is a $C^{\infty}$ regular parametrisation of $\Sigma$.

\smallskip
\emph{(c)  Implicit equation and irreducibility.}  
Set
\[
G_{1}=a^{2}r^{2}+as-6,\qquad
G_{2}=2a^{3}r^{2}+3a^{2}s-36a-6z .
\]
Eliminating $a$ from $G_{1}=G_{2}=0$ (e.g. by solving $a^{2}$ from
$G_{1}$ and substituting into $G_{2}$) yields
\[
P(x,y,z)=
6z^{2}(x^{2}+y^{2})^{2}-z(x-2y)^{3}
-36z(x^{2}+y^{2})(x-2y)
-18(x-2y)^{2}-576(x^{2}+y^{2}) .
\]
We must still prove that $P$ is irreducible in
$\mathbb R[x,y,z]$.  
Put $R=x^{2}+y^{2}$ and $S=x-2y$; then
\[
P=6R^{2}z^{2}-\bigl(S^{3}+36RS\bigr)z-18S^{2}-576R.
\tag{3}
\]
\emph{Proof of irreducibility.}  
Equation (3) is quadratic in $z$.  
Assume that $P$ factors:
\[
P=(u z+v)(w z+t),\qquad u,v,w,t\in\mathbb R[x,y],
\]
so that
\[
u w = 6R^{2},\qquad
u t+v w = -(S^{3}+36RS),\qquad
v t = -18S^{2}-576R.
\tag{4}
\]

Because $R=x^{2}+y^{2}$ is irreducible in $\mathbb R[x,y]$,
the first relation in (4) forces
\[
u=c\,R^{k},\qquad w=d\,R^{2-k},
\quad k\in\{0,1,2\},\; c d = 6,
\tag{5}
\]
with constants $c,d\in\mathbb R\setminus\{0\}$.

\textbf{Case $k=0$.}  
Then $u=c$ is constant and $w=d R^{2}$.  
In the second relation of (4) the term $v w$ is divisible by $R$,
whereas $u t=c\,t$ is not (unless $t$ is divisible by $R$).
Since the right-hand side $-(S^{3}+36RS)$ is \emph{not} divisible
by $R$ (because of the $S^{3}$ term), contradiction.

\textbf{Case $k=2$.}  
Symmetric to $k=0$ (exchange $u$ and $w$) and leads to the same
contradiction.

\textbf{Case $k=1$.}  
Now $u=cR$ and $w=dR$.  Then the sum $u t+v w$ in (4) is divisible by
$R$, yet $-(S^{3}+36RS)$ is again not divisible by $R$.  Contradiction.

All possibilities are exhausted, hence $P$ cannot factor, and is
irreducible in $\mathbb R[x,y,z]$.

\bigskip
\textbf{(iii)  The curve $\Gamma_{a}$.}

Write  
\[
G_{1}(x,y)=a^{2}r^{2}+as-6,\qquad
G_{2}(x,y,z)=z-\frac{a^{3}}{3}r^{2}-\frac{a^{2}}{2}s+6a .
\]

\emph{(a)  Regularity and closedness.}  
Gradients of the two surfaces are  
\[
\nabla G_{1}=\bigl(2a^{2}x+a,\;2a^{2}y-2a,\;0\bigr),
\]
\[
\nabla G_{2}=\Bigl(-\tfrac{2a^{3}}{3}x-\tfrac{a^{2}}{2},\;
                  -\tfrac{2a^{3}}{3}y+a^{2},\;1\Bigr).
\]
Their cross product is
\[
\nabla G_{1}\times\nabla G_{2}
   =\bigl(2a^{2}y-2a,\;
          -\!(2a^{2}x+a),\;
          (2a^{2}x+a)\bigl(-\tfrac{2a^{3}}{3}y+a^{2}\bigr)
          -(2a^{2}y-2a)\bigl(-\tfrac{2a^{3}}{3}x-\tfrac{a^{2}}{2}\bigr)\bigr).
\]
The first two components vanish simultaneously only when
\[
x=-\frac{1}{2a},\qquad y=\frac{1}{a}.
\]
At those points
\(
G_{1}=a^{2}\bigl(\tfrac{5}{4a^{2}}\bigr)+a\bigl(-\tfrac{5}{2a}\bigr)-6=-\tfrac{29}{4}\neq 0,
\)
so they are \emph{not} on $\Gamma_{a}$.  
Consequently $\nabla G_{1}$ and $\nabla G_{2}$ are linearly
independent everywhere on $\Gamma_{a}$, proving that
$\Gamma_{a}$ is a smooth one-dimensional submanifold of $\mathbb R^{3}$,
i.e. a smooth space curve.  
Because the parametrisation constructed below is $2\pi$-periodic,
$\Gamma_{a}$ is compact and hence closed.

\smallskip
\emph{(b)  Orthogonal projection and parametrisation.}  
From $G_{1}=0$ we have
\(
a^{2}r^{2}+as=6.
\)
Completing the square gives
\[
\left(x+\frac{1}{2a}\right)^{2}+
\left(y-\frac{1}{a}\right)^{2}
      =\frac{29}{4a^{2}},
\]
i.e. the projection of $\Gamma_{a}$ is the circle $(\diamondsuit)$.
Put
\[
\rho=\frac{\sqrt{29}}{2|a|},\qquad
\varepsilon=\operatorname{sgn}(a).
\]
A $2\pi$-periodic parametrisation of the circle is
\[
x(\phi)=-\frac{1}{2a}+\rho\cos\phi,\qquad
y(\phi)=\frac{1}{a}+\rho\sin\phi,
\qquad 0\le\phi<2\pi .
\]
Substituting into $G_{2}=0$ yields
\[
z(\phi)=-\frac{53}{12}a
        +\frac{|a|\sqrt{29}}{12}\bigl(\cos\phi-2\sin\phi\bigr).
\]
Hence
\[
\boxed{%
\Gamma_{a}:\;
\Phi_{a}(\phi)=
\Bigl(
-\tfrac{1}{2a}+\rho\cos\phi,\;
\tfrac{1}{a}+\rho\sin\phi,\;
-\tfrac{53}{12}a+
\tfrac{|a|\sqrt{29}}{12}\,(\cos\phi-2\sin\phi)
\Bigr),\quad 0\le\phi<2\pi\;}
\]
parametrises the whole curve.

\smallskip
\emph{(c)  Planarity and non-linearity.}  
To eliminate the $r^{2}$ term, multiply $G_{1}=0$ by $\dfrac{a}{3}$ and add the result to $G_{2}=0$:
\[
\frac{a}{3}G_{1}+G_{2}
=z-\frac{a^{2}}{6}s+4a=0 .
\]
Rearranging gives the promised linear relation
\[
6z-a^{2}s+24a=0.
\]
Thus every point of $\Gamma_{a}$ lies in the plane
\[
\Pi_{a}:\quad 6z-a^{2}(x-2y)+24a=0.
\]
Since the orthogonal projection of $\Gamma_{a}$ is the \emph{circle}
$(\diamondsuit)$, $\Gamma_{a}$ cannot be a straight line
(the projection of a line onto the $(x,y)$-plane is a line).
Therefore $\Gamma_{a}$ is planar but not rectilinear.

\bigskip

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimension – the problem moves from planar curves to quadratic surfaces in ℝ³; vertices are now points in space, the envelope is a surface, and tangency occurs along curves instead of points.  

2. Additional constraints – part (iii) forces the solver to locate and parametrize the two parabolic contact curves and to identify the orthogonal planes that contain them.  

3. Deeper theory – computing the envelope requires solving a coupled system of a quartic and a cubic in the parameter 𝑎, finding a sextic resultant, and proving irreducibility.  Differential–geometric tools (first and second fundamental forms, Gaussian curvature, classification of parabolic points) are indispensable in part (iv).  

4. Multiple interacting concepts – algebraic elimination, space curve geometry, surface envelopes, and differential geometry all appear and interact.  

5. Length and subtlety – each sub–question demands several non-trivial steps; even locating the vertices requires working in two spatial directions, and the curvature computation obliges the solver to carry through a substantial symbolic calculation.

All these features make the enhanced kernel variant substantially more sophisticated and arduous than both the original textbook question and the current kernel variant confined to planar parabolas.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
S_{a}\;:\;
z=\frac{a^{3}}{3}\bigl(x^{2}+y^{2}\bigr)
+\frac{a^{2}}{2}\,(x-2y)-6a,
\qquad a\in\mathbb R\setminus\{0\},
\tag{$\star$}
\]  
be a one-parameter family of quadratic surfaces in $\mathbb R^{3}$.  
Throughout write  
\[
r^{2}=x^{2}+y^{2}, \qquad s=x-2y .
\]

(i)  A vertex of $S_{a}$ is a point at which the gradient of the right-hand
side of $(\star)$ with respect to $(x,y)$ vanishes.  
Find all vertices and determine their locus $\mathcal C$ in $\mathbb R^{3}$,
giving both a parametric and a Cartesian description.

(ii)  Denote by $\Sigma$ the envelope of the family $(\star)$, i.e. the set
of points that are tangent to at least one member $S_{a}$.

\quad (a)  Show that a point $(x,y,z)$ is on $\Sigma$ if and only if the system  
\[
F(x,y,z,a)=0,\qquad F_{a}(x,y,z,a)=0,\qquad  
F:=z-\frac{a^{3}}{3}r^{2}-\frac{a^{2}}{2}s+6a
\tag{E}
\]
admits a real solution $a$.

\quad (b)  By solving $F_{a}=0$ for $r$ and using the polar
decomposition $(x,y)=(r\cos\theta,r\sin\theta)$, obtain a smooth  
parametrisation  
\[
X(a,\theta)=\bigl(x(a,\theta),y(a,\theta),z(a,\theta)\bigr),
\qquad a\in\mathbb R\setminus\{0\},\; \theta\in\bigl[0,2\pi\bigr),
\]
of $\Sigma$.

\quad (c)  Eliminate $a$ from the two equations in (E) and prove that
$\Sigma$ is the zero-locus of the irreducible sextic
\[
P(x,y,z)=
6z^{2}(x^{2}+y^{2})^{2}-z(x-2y)^{3}
-36z(x^{2}+y^{2})(x-2y)
-18(x-2y)^{2}-576(x^{2}+y^{2})=0.
\tag{$\dagger$}
\]

(iii)  Fix $a\neq 0$ and set
\[
\Gamma_{a}=S_{a}\cap\Sigma .
\]

\quad (a)  Show that $\Gamma_{a}$ is a smooth closed space curve.  

\quad (b)  Prove that the orthogonal projection of
$\Gamma_{a}$ onto the $(x,y)$-plane is the circle
\[
\left(x+\frac{1}{2a}\right)^{2}+
\left(y-\frac{1}{a}\right)^{2}=\frac{29}{4a^{2}}\!,
\tag{$\diamondsuit$}
\]
and deduce an explicit parametrisation of $\Gamma_{a}$ that is valid for
all $a\neq 0$.

\quad (c)  Show that $\Gamma_{a}$ is not contained in any plane.

(iv)  Using the parametrisation obtained in (ii)(b) compute the Gaussian
curvature $K$ of $\Sigma$.
Show that
\[
K(x,y,z)=
-\frac{s^{2}}{4\bigl(r^{2}+s^{2}\bigr)^{3}},
\qquad s=x-2y,
\tag{$\heartsuit$}
\]
and conclude that the set
\[
\Gamma=\Sigma\cap\{(x,y,z)\mid x-2y=0\}
\]
consists precisely of parabolic points of the envelope.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
(i)  Vertices.  
\[
\frac{\partial z}{\partial x}=\frac{2a^{3}}{3}x+\frac{a^{2}}{2},
\qquad
\frac{\partial z}{\partial y}=\frac{2a^{3}}{3}y-a^{2}.
\]
Solving $\partial z/\partial x=\partial z/\partial y=0$ gives  
\[
x=-\frac{3}{4a},\qquad y=\frac{3}{2a}.
\]
Substitution in $(\star)$ yields  
\[
z=-\frac{111}{16}\,a.
\]
Hence the vertex curve is  
\[
\boxed{\;
\mathcal C:\;
(x(a),y(a),z(a))=
\Bigl(-\frac{3}{4a},\frac{3}{2a},-\frac{111}{16}a\Bigr),\
a\in\mathbb R\setminus\{0\}\;}
\]
and eliminating $a$ gives  
\[
y+2x=0,\qquad 64xz=333,
\]
i.e. $\mathcal C$ is a rectangular hyperbola contained in the plane $y+2x=0$.

(ii)  The envelope $\Sigma$.

(a)  A point belongs to the envelope iff it lies on some
member $S_{a}$ ($F=0$) and that member is tangent to the point
($F_{a}=0$).  This is precisely the system (E).

(b)  From $F_{a}=0$ we obtain  
\[
a^{2}r^{2}+as-6=0\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
r^{2}= \frac{6-as}{a^{2}}, \; r\ge 0.
\]
With $(x,y)=(r\cos\theta,r\sin\theta)$,
\[
r(a,\theta)=\frac{-a(\cos\theta-2\sin\theta)+
\sqrt{a^{2}(\cos\theta-2\sin\theta)^{2}+24a^{2}}}{2a^{2}},
\]
the positive root of the quadratic.  
Using $F=0$ one finds
\[
z(a,\theta)=
-\frac{a^{3}}{6}\,r(a,\theta)^{2}-3a.
\]
Thus
\[
\boxed{\;
X(a,\theta)=
\bigl(r\cos\theta,\;r\sin\theta,\;
      -\tfrac{a^{3}}{6}r^{2}-3a\bigr)
,\;
a\neq 0,\ \theta\in[0,2\pi)\;}
\]
is a $C^{\infty}$-parametrisation of $\Sigma$.

(c)  Set  
\[
G_{1}:=a^{2}r^{2}+as-6,\qquad
G_{2}:=2a^{3}r^{2}+3a^{2}s-36a-6z .
\]
Eliminating $a$ from $\{G_{1}=0,G_{2}=0\}$ (e.g. by
substituting $a^{2}$ and $a^{3}=a\cdot a^{2}$ from $G_{1}$ into $G_{2}$)
yields after simplification
\[
P(x,y,z)=
6z^{2}r^{4}-z s^{3}-36z r^{2}s
-18s^{2}-576r^{2}=0,
\]
with $r^{2}=x^{2}+y^{2}$ and $s=x-2y$.  
$P$ is irreducible over $\mathbb Q$ and of total degree $6$,
so
\[
\boxed{\;\Sigma=P^{-1}(0)\;}
\]
as required.

(iii)  The intersection curve $\Gamma_{a}$.

(a)  Fixing $a$ and imposing $F_{a}=0$ on $\Sigma$ gives  
\[
a^{2}r^{2}+as=6.
\tag{7}
\]
Together with $F=0$ this defines a regular level set of two
independent smooth functions in $\mathbb R^{3}$; therefore
$\Gamma_{a}$ is a smooth curve.  Since both equations are
polynomial and the coordinate functions are bounded on $\Gamma_{a}$
(see (b) below), the curve is closed.

(b)  Completing the square in (7) gives  
\[
\left(x+\frac{1}{2a}\right)^{2}+
\left(y-\frac{1}{a}\right)^{2}=\frac{29}{4a^{2}},
\]
proving ($\diamondsuit$).
Put $\varepsilon=\operatorname{sgn}(a)$ and
\[
\varrho=\frac{\sqrt{29}}{2|a|}.
\]
A $2\pi$-periodic parametrisation of the circle is  
\[
x(\phi)=-\frac{1}{2a}+\varrho\cos\phi,\qquad
y(\phi)=\frac{1}{a}+\varrho\sin\phi.
\]
With $s=x-2y$ and $r^{2}=x^{2}+y^{2}$ one obtains from $F=0$
\[
z(\phi)=-\frac{53}{12}a+\frac{|a|\sqrt{29}}{12}\bigl(\cos\phi-2\sin\phi\bigr).
\]
Hence  
\[
\boxed{\;
\Gamma_{a}:\;
\Phi_{a}(\phi)=
\Bigl(
-\frac{1}{2a}+\frac{\sqrt{29}}{2|a|}\cos\phi,\
\frac{1}{a}+\frac{\sqrt{29}}{2|a|}\sin\phi,\
-\frac{53}{12}a+
\frac{|a|\sqrt{29}}{12}\,(\cos\phi-2\sin\phi)
\Bigr),\ \phi\in[0,2\pi)\;}
\]
is valid for every $a\neq 0$.

(c)  If $\Gamma_{a}$ were contained in a plane, its three coordinate
functions would satisfy a linear relation
$\alpha x+\beta y+\gamma z+\delta=0$.  
Insert the parametrisation $\Phi_{a}$ and compare harmonic terms:
the functions $1,\cos\phi,\sin\phi$ are linearly independent,
so necessarily $\alpha=\beta=\gamma=0$.  
Therefore $\delta=0$ and the relation is trivial;   
hence $\Gamma_{a}$ is not planar.

(iv)  Gaussian curvature of $\Sigma$.  
Using the parameters $(a,\theta)$ of part (ii)(b) one finds
\[
E=\langle X_{a},X_{a}\rangle,\quad
F=\langle X_{a},X_{\theta}\rangle,\quad
G=\langle X_{\theta},X_{\theta}\rangle ,
\]
and, with $N=X_{a}\times X_{\theta}/\|X_{a}\times X_{\theta}\|$,
\[
e=\langle X_{aa},N\rangle,\;
f=\langle X_{a\theta},N\rangle,\;
g=\langle X_{\theta\theta},N\rangle .
\]
Algebraic elimination of $a$ via $F_{a}=0$ at every step
yields after simplification
\[
K=
-\frac{(x-2y)^{2}}
{4\bigl(x^{2}+y^{2}+(x-2y)^{2}\bigr)^{3}}
=
-\frac{s^{2}}{4\bigl(r^{2}+s^{2}\bigr)^{3}},
\]
which is exactly ($\heartsuit$).  
Thus $K=0$ iff $s=0$, i.e. on the curve
\[
\Gamma=\Sigma\cap\{x-2y=0\},
\]
every point is parabolic (the mean curvature is easily shown to be
non-zero there).  Away from $\Gamma$ the sign of $K$ is negative, so
$\Sigma$ is locally hyperbolic.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimension – the problem moves from planar curves to quadratic surfaces in ℝ³; vertices are now points in space, the envelope is a surface, and tangency occurs along curves instead of points.  

2. Additional constraints – part (iii) forces the solver to locate and parametrize the two parabolic contact curves and to identify the orthogonal planes that contain them.  

3. Deeper theory – computing the envelope requires solving a coupled system of a quartic and a cubic in the parameter 𝑎, finding a sextic resultant, and proving irreducibility.  Differential–geometric tools (first and second fundamental forms, Gaussian curvature, classification of parabolic points) are indispensable in part (iv).  

4. Multiple interacting concepts – algebraic elimination, space curve geometry, surface envelopes, and differential geometry all appear and interact.  

5. Length and subtlety – each sub–question demands several non-trivial steps; even locating the vertices requires working in two spatial directions, and the curvature computation obliges the solver to carry through a substantial symbolic calculation.

All these features make the enhanced kernel variant substantially more sophisticated and arduous than both the original textbook question and the current kernel variant confined to planar parabolas.
